text.title: Change Control Antipatterns and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Common change control failures, process traps, and agent-specific pitfalls for automated change request handling and impact analysis.

antipattern.description: No formal process — changes happen informally via chat and verbal agreements.
antipattern.reason: Informal changes have no impact analysis, no audit trail, and no baseline update. They compound invisibly until the schedule or budget collapses with no documented cause.

antipattern.description: PM approves every change regardless of size, bypassing Sponsor and CCB.
antipattern.reason: PM authority is limited by tier. Overreach on major changes removes the sponsor's ability to protect the project's ROI case and creates contractual exposure.

antipattern.description: Implementing an approved CR without updating the scope, schedule, and budget baselines simultaneously.
antipattern.reason: Baselines that lag behind approved changes make EVM calculations and future change impact analysis inaccurate. Update baselines atomically with implementation confirmation.

antipattern.description: Rejecting changes without documenting the reason.
antipattern.reason: Undocumented rejections allow the same CR to re-appear under a different title. A rejection log with reasoning enables pattern detection and deflects repeat requests with evidence.

rule.statement: Require the agent's impact analysis to walk specific WBS node IDs, not handwave "schedule impact".
rule.rationale: LLMs underestimate ripple effects because they reason at the summary level. Walking specific WBS elements forces the agent to consider dependency chains and resource conflicts that summary-level analysis misses.

list.item: Agents recommend Option A (full implementation) by default; explicitly require ranking all options including defer and decline
Generated business justifications paraphrase the request instead of citing metrics; force quoting stakeholder evidence or measurement data
"Approval drift" via Slack threads: agent must refuse to mark a CR Approved without an explicit decision artefact (signed form, ticket transition, email sign-off)
Agents merge or rebase implementation branches without confirming baseline updates; add a checklist gate before branch merge
Set a CR rate metric: > 1 CR/week per $1M scope = symptom of poor initial scoping; surface to sponsor rather than silently processing
Near-duplicate CRs: compare new CR against last 90 days of rejected CRs; flag similarity > 0.7 with prior rejection reasons
